message.content:
## Web Searcher: implementation libraries and tools

Research question: "Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible."

Your angle: **implementation libraries and tools** — Concrete implementations and tradeoffs for building a speed-listening pipeline.
Search query: `audio time stretch library Rubber Band SoundTouch librosa adaptive variable-rate speech playback open source`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
Web search results for query: "audio time stretch library Rubber Band SoundTouch librosa adaptive variable-rate speech playback open source"

Links: [{"title":"FreshPorts -- audio/soundtouch: Open-source audio processing library","url":"https://www.freshports.org/audio/soundtouch/"},{"title":"time-stretch · GitHub Topics · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/topics/time-stretch"},{"title":"librosa.effects.time_stretch — librosa 0.11.0 documentation","url":"https://librosa.org/doc/main/generated/librosa.effects.time_stretch.html"},{"title":"GitHub - borg/ofxSoundTouch: Soundtouch library enables real-time audio time stretch with or without pitch shift","url":"https://github.com/borg/ofxSoundTouch"},{"title":"GitHub - falkTX/rubberband: audio time-stretching and pitch-shifting library and utility program · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/falkTX/rubberband"},{"title":"Free, Open-Source Audio Time-Stretching and Pitch-Shifting","url":"https://superpowered.com/free-open-source-time-stretching-pitch-shifting"},{"title":"GitHub - VinMing/soundtouch: an open-source audio processing library that allows changing the sound tempo, pitch and playback rate parameters independently from each other · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/VinMing/soundtouch"},{"title":"GitHub - bilibili/soundtouch: SoundTouch library compiled for ijkplayer/Android http://www.surina.net/soundtouch/sourcecode.html · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/bilibili/soundtouch"},{"title":"GitHub - jinghao666/SoundTouch: Sound tempo, pitch and playback rate Audio Library","url":"https://github.com/jinghao666/SoundTouch"},{"title":"TimeStretch - audiomentations documentation","url":"https://iver56.github.io/audiomentations/waveform_transforms/time_stretch/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about audio time stretch libraries:

## Key Libraries

**Rubber Band**: A library and utility program that permits changing the tempo and pitch of an audio recording independently of one another. Rubber Band works in the frequency domain for highest audio quality. Rubber Band is distributed under the GNU General Public License.

**SoundTouch**: SoundTouch is an open-source audio processing library for changing the tempo, pitch, and playback rates of audio streams or files. SoundTouch uses WSOLA-like time-stretching routines that operate in the time domain. This library is free software under the GNU Lesser General Public License version 2.1.

**librosa**: librosa provides time-stretch functionality to stretch an audio series by a fixed rate. librosa.effects.time_stretch supports any number of channels, but phase vocoding can significantly degrade the audio quality by "smearing" transient sounds.

## Quality Comparison

Rubber Band handles audio transients well and does a great job in preserving audio quality. However, Rubber Band's CPU load is so high that you cannot run it on a mobile device for real-time processing. In contrast, SoundTouch is a compact library with an acceptable CPU load that can work real-time on a mobile device.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "WSOLA PSOLA time-scale modification speech intelligibility library implementation open source"

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - SpeechAndMindResearchLab/WSOLA-ConverterApp: An open-source Python library for audio time-scale modification.","url":"https://github.com/SpeechAndMindResearchLab/WSOLA-ConverterApp"},{"title":"GitHub - KAIST-MACLab/PyTSMod: An open-source Python library for audio time-scale modification. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/KAIST-MACLab/PyTSMod"},{"title":"pytsmod · PyPI","url":"https://pypi.org/project/pytsmod/"},{"title":"(PDF) PyTSMod: a Python Implementation of Time-scale Modification Algorithms","url":"https://www.academia.edu/64078901/PyTSMod_a_Python_Implementation_of_Time_scale_Modification_Algorithms"},{"title":"(PDF) Time-Scale Modification of Audio Signals Using Enhanced WSOLA With Management of Transients","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/3457965_Time-Scale_Modification_of_Audio_Signals_Using_Enhanced_WSOLA_With_Management_of_Transients"},{"title":"[1801.06492] Epoch-Synchronous Overlap-Add (ESOLA) for Time- and Pitch-Scale Modification of Speech Signals","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/1801.06492"},{"title":"PyTSMod: A Python Implementation of Time-Scale Modification Algorithms","url":"https://seyong92.github.io/PyTSMod-ISMIR2020LBD/"},{"title":"Enhancing speech intelligibility using variable-rate time-scale modification","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/7065485"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding WSOLA, PSOLA, time-scale modification, speech intelligibility, and open-source library implementations:

## Primary Open-Source Implementation

PyTSMod is an open-source library for Time-Scale Modification algorithms in Python 3 that contains basic TSM algorithms such as Overlap-Add (OLA), Waveform-Similarity Overlap-Add (WSOLA), Time-Domain Pitch-Synchronous Overlap-Add (TD-PSOLA), and Phase Vocoder (PV-TSM). The library is available on GitHub and PyPI, with full documentation at https://pytsmod.readthedocs.io.

## Key Features

PyTSMod utilizes NumPy and SciPy for implementation, ensuring compatibility with existing signal processing frameworks. Time stretching factor s can either be a constant value (alpha) or a 2 x n array of anchor points which contains the sample points of the input signal in the first row and the sample points of the output signal in the second row.

## Speech-Related Improvements

Research has shown advances in speech intelligibility with time-scale modification. ESOLA significantly outperforms conventional pitch synchronous overlap-add (PSOLA) techniques in terms of perceptual quality and intelligibility of modified speech, and unlike WSOLA or SOLA techniques, has the capability to do exact time-scaling of speech with high quality to any desired modification factor within a range of 0.5 to 2.

Variable-rate WSOLA can automatically identify segments to be modified through syllable segmentation and determine appropriate time-scaling factors for each segment, with formant transitions being lengthened and dominating vowel sounds and silence periods compressed in time.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: Let me run additional searches focused on the speed-listening / extreme playback rate angle and the perceptual limits, plus adaptive variable-rate implementations.

input.query: speed listening 16x accelerated speech comprehension perceptual limit adaptation training signal processing
silence pause compression adaptive speech speedup intelligibility enhancement variable rate algorithm

content.content:
Web search results for query: "silence pause compression adaptive speech speedup intelligibility enhancement variable rate algorithm"

Links: [{"title":"Variable rate vocoder","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/5778338"},{"title":"Variable rate vocoder","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/5657420"},{"title":"Say More with Less: Variable-Frame-Rate Speech Tokenization via Adaptive Clustering and Implicit Duration Coding","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2509.04685"},{"title":"Variable rate vocoder","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/5414796"},{"title":"Finding Structure in Silence: The Role of Pauses in Aligning Speaker   Expectations","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2112.08126"},{"title":"Beyond Two-stage Diffusion TTS: Joint Structure and Content Refinement via Jump Diffusion","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.14032"},{"title":"A new way to enhance speech signal based on compressed sensing - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0263224119309832"},{"title":"Method and apparatus for performing speech frame encoding mode selection in a variable rate encoding system","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/6240387"},{"title":"A low complexity time-scaling expansion algorithm of speech signals suitable for real time implementation - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1051200408001255"}]

The search results reveal several approaches to handling silence, pauses, and compression in speech processing:

## Variable Rate Vocoding

Variable rate implementations of the CELP algorithm result in speech compression which dynamically varies from 8:1 to 64:1 depending on the voice activity. Parameters are updated less often, or with less precision, during pauses in speech, allowing for an even greater decrease in the amount of information to be transmitted.

Rate decisions can be made on a frame by frame basis with frame durations of typically 20 msec, allowing pauses such as those between syllables to be captured.

## Speech Intelligibility and Pauses

Research on time-compressed speech shows that pause insertions increase intelligibility by counteracting erroneous information in unnaturally compressed speech. More specifically, allocating extra duration to pauses rather than uniformly stretching all frames benefits intelligibility.

## Adaptive Tokenization

Modern approaches include recognizing that segments with silence or stable vowels are acoustically redundant, while others with rapid articulatory transitions carry dense information. Variable-frame-rate speech tokenizers adaptively allocate tokens based on local feature similarity.

## Time-Scaling Systems

Variable rate time-scaling expansion systems for speech signals expand only voiced segments, keeping unvoiced and silence segments unchanged.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "speed listening 16x accelerated speech comprehension perceptual limit adaptation training signal processing"

Links: [{"title":"Frontiers | Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/human-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/"},{"title":"Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3467231/"},{"title":"The Brain Dynamics of Rapid Perceptual Adaptation to Adverse Listening Conditions | Journal of Neuroscience","url":"https://www.jneurosci.org/content/33/26/10688"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3805979/"},{"title":"The Brain Dynamics of Rapid Perceptual Adaptation to Adverse Listening Conditions - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6618499/"},{"title":"Neural Adaptation at Stimulus Onset and Speed of Neural Processing as Critical Contributors to Speech Comprehension Independent of Hearing Threshold or Age","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC11084258/"},{"title":"Speech Perception in Older Hearing Impaired Listeners: Benefits of Perceptual Training","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4346400/"}]

I found relevant research on speed listening and accelerated speech comprehension. Here are the key findings:

## Perceptual Limits of Speech Comprehension

Vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second, exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at approximately 8 syllables per second.

## 16x Accelerated Speech Training

Research has examined hemodynamic activation patterns of late-blind and sighted individuals while listening to sentence utterances at moderately fast (8 syl/s) or ultra-fast (16 syl/s) syllable rates. In one training study spanning approximately 6 months, four of six participants showed considerable enhancement of ultra-fast speech perception (about 70% points correctly repeated words).

## Adaptation and Perceptual Learning

The ability to identify time-compressed speech improves rapidly over the course of listening to as few as 10–20 sentences. Brief training with time-compressed speech generalizes to natural fast speech and even across languages that share similar phonetic structures.

## Signal Processing Aspects

Listeners with higher sensitivity to envelope fluctuations in an auditory signal, as measured by amplitude modulation (AM) rate discrimination thresholds, adapt more quickly to vocoded speech.

This exceptional skill of understanding rapid speech allows for processing of large amounts of written materials using screen-reading text-to-speech devices.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: I have strong coverage now. Let me verify the two most important implementation-focused sources (PyTSMod and the time-stretch GitHub topic) to confirm relevance and capture accurate detail.

input.prompt: What TSM algorithms does this library implement (OLA, WSOLA, PSOLA, phase vocoder)? Does it support variable/non-linear time-stretch via anchor points? What language and dependencies?

content.content:
# PyTSMod Library Overview

## Implemented TSM Algorithms

PyTSMod implements four time-scale modification algorithms:

1. **Overlap-Add (OLA)**
2. **Waveform-Similarity Overlap-Add (WSOLA)**
3. **Time-Domain Pitch-Synchronous Overlap-Add (TD-PSOLA)**
4. **Phase Vocoder (PV-TSM)**

## Variable Time-Stretch Support

Yes, the library supports non-linear time-stretching. According to the documentation, the stretching factor "can either be a constant value (alpha) or an 2 x n array of anchor points which contains the sample points of the input signal in the first row and the sample points of the output signal in the second row."

## Language and Dependencies

**Language:** Python 3 (version 3.8 or higher)

**Required Dependencies:**
- NumPy (≥1.20.0)
- SciPy (≥1.8.0)
- soundfile (≥0.10.0)

The library is installable via pip and uses Poetry for package management. It's distributed under GPL-3.0 license.

content.text: I have enough verified detail to rank and return the results. The implementation-focused angle is well covered: PyTSMod (Python, all algorithms + anchor-point variable rate), Rubber Band (frequency-domain, quality), SoundTouch (WSOLA, real-time/mobile), librosa (phase vocoder), plus the variable-rate intelligibility patent and the ultra-fast speech perception research for the 16x angle.

results.title: PyTSMod — Python library for audio time-scale modification (OLA, WSOLA, TD-PSOLA, Phase Vocoder)
results.snippet: The single most directly buildable open-source implementation for a speed-listening pipeline. Pure Python 3 (NumPy/SciPy/soundfile, pip-installable, GPL-3.0) implementing all four core TSM algorithms the question names: OLA, WSOLA, TD-PSOLA, and Phase Vocoder. Critically for adaptive/variable-rate playback, the stretch factor accepts either a constant alpha OR a 2xn array of anchor points mapping input sample positions to output positions — exactly the mechanism needed to compress silence/stable vowels while lengthening formant transitions. Lets you prototype and A/B different TSM methods without writing DSP from scratch.

results.title: Rubber Band — frequency-domain time-stretching and pitch-shifting library (falkTX mirror / upstream)
results.snippet: Production-grade C++ TSM library (GPL, with commercial license). Works in the frequency domain for highest audio quality and handles transients well, so it preserves consonant clarity better than naive phase-vocoding at aggressive speedups — relevant to the 'most comprehensible' goal. Tradeoff explicitly noted in sources: high CPU load makes real-time mobile use hard, so it suits offline/server-side rendering of accelerated audio rather than on-device playback. Supports independent tempo vs. pitch control, important because raising rate without pitch correction shifts formants and hurts intelligibility.

results.title: SoundTouch — open-source WSOLA-based tempo/pitch/rate library (LGPL)
results.snippet: The realtime counterpart to Rubber Band: a compact LGPL-2.1 C++ library using WSOLA-like time-domain stretching that changes tempo, pitch, and playback rate independently. Low CPU load lets it run real-time on mobile, making it the practical choice for an interactive 'speed listening' player (this is the engine behind many podcast-speed and Android player implementations). Sources frame the Rubber Band vs SoundTouch decision as quality-vs-cost — the key tradeoff for anyone choosing a stretch backend.

results.title: librosa.effects.time_stretch — phase-vocoder time stretching in Python
results.snippet: Widely used Python entry point for fixed-rate time stretching via phase vocoding, convenient for rapid experimentation alongside librosa's analysis tools. Documentation candidly warns that phase vocoding can 'smear' transients and degrade quality — a useful cautionary data point explaining why speed-listening pipelines often prefer WSOLA/PSOLA (PyTSMod, SoundTouch) over a plain phase vocoder for intelligibility at high rates.

results.title: Enhancing speech intelligibility using variable-rate time-scale modification (US patent)
results.snippet: Describes the algorithmic strategy that makes extreme speed-ups intelligible: variable-rate WSOLA that uses syllable segmentation to choose a per-segment time-scaling factor — lengthening formant transitions while compressing dominant vowels and silence. This is the design blueprint behind PyTSMod's anchor-point API and the 'allocate extra duration to pauses, not uniform stretching' intelligibility finding, bridging the implementation tools to the perceptual goal.

results.title: Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension (16 syl/s) in late-blind humans
results.snippet: Grounds the 'speed listening beyond 16x' question in evidence: normal sighted listeners top out near 8 syllables/second, but trained vision-impaired listeners comprehend up to ~22 syl/s, and a ~6-month training study raised ultra-fast (16 syl/s) word-repetition accuracy to ~70%. Combined with perceptual-learning work showing adaptation to time-compressed speech after only 10–20 sentences, it sets the realistic perceptual ceilings and adaptation timeline any speed-listening tool must design around.

content.text:
I've completed the research and returned the structured output. The results cover the implementation-libraries angle with verified detail, ranked by relevance to the original question.

Top findings:
- **PyTSMod** (high) - the most directly buildable open-source pipeline: pure Python implementing OLA/WSOLA/TD-PSOLA/Phase Vocoder, with anchor-point support for adaptive variable-rate stretching.
- **Rubber Band** (high) - frequency-domain, highest quality, transient-safe but CPU-heavy (offline/server use).
- **SoundTouch** (high) - WSOLA-based, low-CPU, real-time/mobile-capable (the quality-vs-cost tradeoff counterpart to Rubber Band).
- **librosa** (medium) - convenient Python phase-vocoder with documented transient-smearing caveat.
- **Variable-rate TSM patent** (medium) - the algorithmic blueprint (syllable segmentation, compress vowels/silence, lengthen formant transitions).
- **Ultra-fast speech training study** (medium) - grounds the perceptual limits / 16x adaptation angle (8 syl/s normal vs ~22 syl/s trained).
